source.title: ANF06. Fathers of the Third Century: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Dionysius the Great, Julius Africanus, Anatolius, and Minor Writers, Methodius, Arnobius

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: No one, however, has ever made such an unblushing advance in the promulgation of these tenets as this Scythianus.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For he introduced the notion of a feud between the two unbegottens, and all those other fancies which are the consequences of a position of that kind.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: This Scythianus himself belonged to the stock of the Saracens, and took as his wife a certain captive from the Upper Thebaid, who persuaded him to dwell in Egypt rather than in the deserts.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And would that he had never been received by that province, in which, as he dwelt in it for a period, he found the opportunity for learning the wisdom of the Egyptians! for, to speak truth, he was a person of very decided talent, and also of very liberal means, as those who knew him have likewise testified in accounts transmitted to us.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Moreover, he had a certain disciple named Terebinthus, who wrote four books for him.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: To the first of these books he gave the title of the Mysteries, to the second that of the Heads, to the third that of the Gospel, and to the last of all that of the Treasury.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: He had these four books, and this one disciple whose name was Terebinthus.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: As, then, these two persons had determined to reside alone by themselves for a considerable period, Scythianus thought of making an excursion into Judea, with the purpose of meeting with all those who had a reputation there as teachers; but it came to pass that he suddenly departed this life soon after that, without having been able to accomplish anything.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: That disciple, moreover, who had sojourned with him had to flee, and made his way toward Babylonia, a province which at present is held by the Persians, and which is distant now a journey of about six days and nights from our parts.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: On arriving there, Terebinthus succeeded in giving currency to a wonderful account of himself, declaring that he was replete with all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and that he was really named now, not Terebinthus, but another Buddas, and that this designation had been put upon him.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: He asserted further that he was the son of a certain virgin, and that he had been brought up by an angel on the mountains.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: A certain prophet, however, of the name of Parcus, and Labdacus the son of Mithras, charged him with falsehood, and day after day unceasingly they had keen and elevated contentions on this subject.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But why should I speak of that at length?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Although he was often reproved, he continued, nevertheless, to make declarations to them on matters which were antecedent to the world, and on the sphere, and the two luminaries; and also on the question whither and in what manner the souls depart, and in what mode they return again into the bodies; and he made many other assertions of this nature, and others even worse than these,—as, for instance, that war was raised with God among the elements, that the prophet himself might be believed.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: However, as he was hard pressed for assertions like these, he betook himself to a certain widow, along with his four books: for he had attached to himself no disciple in that same locality, with the single exception of an old woman who became an intimate of his.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Then, on a subsequent occasion, at the earliest dawn one morning, he went up to the top of a certain house, and there began to invoke certain names, which Turbo has told us only the seven elect have learned.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: He ascended to the housetop, then, with the purpose of engaging in some religious ceremony, or some art of his own; and he went up alone, so as not to be detected by any one: for he considered that, if he was convicted of playing false with, or holding of little account, the religious beliefs of the people, he would be liable to be punished by the real princes of the country.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And as he was revolving these things then in his mind, God in His perfect justice decreed that he should be thrust beneath earth by a spirit; and forthwith he was cast down from the roof of the house; and his body, being precipitated lifeless to the ground, was taken up in pity by the old woman mentioned above, and was buried in the wonted place of sepulture.
